title: Puttanesca
subtitle: Bucatini with olives, capers, anchovies, and tomato
excerpt: A bold, punchy sauce of tinned tomatoes, black olives, capers, and anchovies tossed through bucatini with garlic and chilli. The Neapolitan pasta that assembles in 20 minutes from pantry staples — intensely savoury, with no need for cheese.
sourceNotes: Based on the Neapolitan puttanesca tradition as documented in Marcella Hazan, 'Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking' (1992) and Anna Del Conte, 'Gastronomy of Italy' (1987). The dish is of twentieth-century origin, first documented in Naples in the 1960s.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Sauce keeps refrigerated for 3 days. Cook pasta fresh.

content.text: Puttanesca is a pantry pasta — it demands nothing fresh beyond the pasta itself, and the sauce takes roughly the same time to make as the pasta takes to boil. The anchovies dissolve entirely in the oil and provide the underlying savouriness without being detectable as fish; the olives and capers give salt and acid; the tinned tomatoes supply body. The result is a sharp, complex sauce with considerable depth for something assembled in 20 minutes.

Bucatini (thick, hollow spaghetti) is the traditional pasta for puttanesca in Naples, and the hollow centre traps the sauce in a way that spaghetti does not. Spaghetti or rigatoni are perfectly good substitutes. No cheese: puttanesca is not finished with parmesan. The sauce is salty enough without it, and cheese clashes with the anchovies.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over a medium heat. Add {{anchovy-fillets}} anchovies and {{garlic}} sliced garlic. Stir until the anchovies dissolve into the oil, about 2 minutes. Add {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes, then {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes. Crush the tomatoes with the back of a spoon. Simmer for 10 minutes until the sauce reduces slightly. Add {{black-olives}} of olives and {{capers}} of capers. Cook for 3 more minutes.

Cook {{spaghetti}} of spaghetti or bucatini in a large pan of well-salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve a mug of pasta water, then drain. Add the pasta to the sauce and toss over a medium heat for 1 minute, adding pasta water as needed to loosen. Scatter {{parsley}} of chopped parsley and serve immediately.

items.cause: Anchovies, olives, and capers are all salty; pasta water adds more if oversalted
items.fix: Rinse the capers well before adding. Taste the sauce before seasoning the pasta water heavily. Add a small amount of pasta water to dilute if needed.

items.symptom: Anchovies still visible in the sauce
items.cause: Heat too low when added, or not stirred enough
items.fix: The anchovies should dissolve completely in the warm oil. Stir actively and use medium heat.

content.text: Puttanesca is a Neapolitan invention of the mid-twentieth century — the name (from puttana, meaning prostitute) is explained by various stories, most of them implausible, about the dish being quick to make between clients or thrown together from larder scraps. Its actual origin is more mundane: it is a resourceful poor kitchen preparation, built entirely from preserved ingredients that were available year-round. The dish spread rapidly through Italian cooking from the 1970s and appears in every major Italian cookbook of the last 50 years.
